title: Fish and Chips
subtitle: Battered cod or haddock with thick-cut chips, fried in the same oil
excerpt: Fish and chips made at home requires a deep pan, a probe thermometer, and enough oil to cover the fish. The batter should be cold and thin enough to coat in a single pass; a warm or thick batter produces a doughy crust rather than the light, bubbly shell of a good chippie. Chips go in first at 130°C to cook through, then the temperature is raised to 190°C for the fish and to finish the chips.
sourceNotes: Fish and chips became the defining British takeaway dish from the 1860s, when fried fish sellers from the Jewish East End community and chip sellers from the industrial North began combining their offerings. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) documents fried fish in batter. The combination of battered fish and fried chips was established by the 1870s as the standard dish. Classic British home recreation of the chip-shop method.

content.text: is what separates properly cooked chips from those that are dark on the outside and undercooked inside. The first fry at 130°C takes 6 to 8 minutes; it cooks the potato through. The second fry at 190°C takes 2 to 3 minutes and gives the colour and crispness. The fish goes in at 190°C after the chips are removed for their second fry. The
must be made 30 minutes before using and kept cold; warm batter does not bubble and crisp in the same way.

items.name: Cod fillet
items.prepNote: approx. 180 g each, skin on or off

items.name: Plain flour
items.prepNote: plus extra for dusting the fish

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: Maris Piper, approx. 200 g each, peeled and cut into 1.5 cm chips

items.name: Sunflower oil
items.prepNote: or vegetable oil, for deep frying

content.text: Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{beer}} of cold lager together until a smooth batter forms. It should coat the back of a spoon with a thin, even layer. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a large, deep saucepan to 130°C. Add the {{potato}} chips in batches; do not crowd the pan. Fry for 6 to 8 minutes until cooked through but pale. Remove with a slotted spoon to a tray lined with kitchen paper. The chips will not be golden at this stage.
Fry the fish and finish the chips

Raise the oil temperature to 190°C. Dust each {{cod-fillet}} cod fillet in plain flour, then dip in the cold batter, letting the excess drip back into the bowl. Lower into the oil away from you. Fry for 4 to 5 minutes per side until the batter is golden-brown and the fish is cooked through. Keep warm in a low oven. Return the chips to the 190°C oil and fry for 2 to 3 minutes until crisp and golden. Season with salt and serve immediately.

rows.fix: Pat the fish completely dry before dusting in flour. The flour gives the batter something to grip. Batter should be thin enough to pour freely.
rows.cause: Fish not dried, or batter too thick
rows.symptom: Batter falling off the fish during frying

rows.fix: Use a thermometer; do not estimate the oil temperature. Keep the batter refrigerated until use. Cold batter hitting hot oil is what creates the crust.
rows.cause: Oil not hot enough, or batter not cold

rows.fix: Ensure the oil is back at 190°C before adding the chips for the second fry. Crowded chips bring the temperature down; fry in small batches.
rows.cause: Oil temperature too low for second fry
rows.symptom: Chips pale even after second fry

content.text: Fish and chips is the defining British takeaway dish. The home version is a project rather than a weeknight dish; the results are best eaten immediately from newspaper, if you are committed to the tradition, or from a warm plate.
